question: Define an entity set and explain how it differs from an entity. Provide a concrete example of each.
expectedPoints: An entity is a real-world object or concept that can be distinctly identified (e.g., a specific student named 'John Doe' with ID 12345).
An entity set is a collection of entities of the same type that share the same attributes (e.g., the set of all 'Students' in a university).
The key difference: an entity is an instance or occurrence, while an entity set is the 'type' or 'collection' of those instances.

question: Describe the two main types of structural constraints in an ER model, explaining how they are represented and their purpose in defining relationships.
expectedPoints: Mapping Cardinalities: Define the number of entities to which another entity can be associated via a relationship. The main types are one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many.

Participation Constraints: Define whether the existence of an entity depends on its association with another entity via a relationship. Total participation (existence dependency) is shown with a double line, meaning every entity must participate. Partial participation is shown with a single line.
Together, these constraints precisely define the business rules that the database schema must enforce, ensuring data integrity.

question: Analyze a scenario where a university needs to model the relationship between 'Instructors' and 'Courses'. An instructor can teach many courses, but a course must be taught by exactly one instructor. A new instructor might be hired who is not yet assigned to teach any course. Create and fully explain the ER diagram for this relationship, including all appropriate constraints, and justify your design choices.
expectedPoints: The relationship from 'Instructors' to 'Courses' is 'Teaches'. The cardinality is one-to-many from the 'Instructors' side to the 'Courses' side.

The participation constraint for 'Courses' is total (existence dependency), as a course MUST be taught by an instructor. This is represented by a double line from the 'Courses' entity to the 'Teaches' relationship.

The participation constraint for 'Instructors' is partial, as an instructor can exist in the system without teaching any courses (e.g., a newly hired professor). This is represented by a single line from the 'Instructors' entity to the 'Teaches' relationship.

Justification: The design accurately reflects the business rules: a course cannot exist without an assigned instructor (preventing 'orphan' courses), while allowing flexibility for instructors who are not currently teaching.
